title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered with a food bank delivery service. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Marta, The Packing Supervisor
Marta began packing food parcels after her office introduced a four-day week. [47]She soon realised that the most important job was checking dietary labels before tins and packets disappeared into identical cardboard boxes.[/47] Her experience managing stock in a pharmacy helped her rotate dates and separate items that contained common allergens. [48]She designed a checklist that volunteers had to sign, reducing the number of parcels that needed to be reopened at the last minute.[/48] Marta found the work emotionally harder than expected because the shelves looked full while some requests still could not be met. The best days were when local gardeners brought fresh vegetables that changed a box from emergency supplies into something like a meal. She now believes dignity lies in details: a birthday cake mix for a child, tea for an elderly person, or spices for someone missing familiar food in a new country or a difficult week.
texts.B:
### Simon, The Route Planner
Simon did not meet many recipients, but he planned the weekly delivery routes from a donated desk near the loading bay. [49]He had assumed the quickest route would be best, until he learned that some people needed parcels delivered after medical appointments or before children came home.[/49] His background as a taxi dispatcher helped him think beyond maps and include timing, lifts, roadworks, parking restrictions and drivers' confidence. [50]The most awkward mistake happened when two similar addresses on the same estate were confused, leaving one volunteer knocking at an empty flat.[/50] After that, he added landmarks to every route sheet. Simon says delivery work changed his idea of efficiency: a route is successful only if the right person receives the right parcel discreetly. He keeps a pencil nearby during every shift, because one phone call can reorganise the entire evening, especially when a driver gets lost or a lift breaks down.
texts.C:
### Iqra, The Phone Volunteer
Iqra answered calls from people requesting help, a role she accepted because she spoke several community languages and had a calm manner. [51]She learned to ask necessary questions without sounding as if she were testing whether callers deserved support.[/51] The first weeks left her drained after every shift, especially when people sometimes apologised repeatedly for needing food. Her previous work in customer service helped her keep her voice steady while entering information accurately, even during long pauses. [52]One caller cried with relief when Iqra mentioned baby formula before he had found the courage to ask for it.[/52] That moment taught her that listening includes noticing what has not yet been said. [55]Iqra also created translated notes explaining collection times, because relatives often shared information across households.[/55] She says the phone desk is where practical help begins to feel human rather than official, especially during a crisis that already feels humiliating.
texts.D:
### Rob, The Van Loader
Rob joined the delivery team because he owned a large blue van and thought carrying boxes would be simple after years of weekend driving. [53]He quickly discovered that loading order mattered, since the first parcels needed to be placed nearest the doors, not buried under heavier crates.[/53] Years of moving musical equipment for bands helped him balance weight and protect fragile items, including eggs and donated fruit. [54]His worst moment was breaking a jar of pasta sauce, which soaked through several paper bags and delayed three deliveries.[/54] He felt foolish, but the accident made the team switch to stronger crates for liquids and tape the lids of donated jars. [56]Rob rarely hears the stories behind the parcels, and he prefers it that way because it protects recipients' privacy.[/56] Still, he takes pride in lifting carefully, knowing that respect can be shown before anyone opens the door or knows his name.

questions.question: says checking food information before packing was especially important?
questions.explanation: Marta had to check dietary labels before items went into boxes.

questions.question: introduced a signed checklist to reduce last-minute corrections?
questions.explanation: Marta's checklist reduced parcels that had to be reopened.

questions.question: learned that delivery timing could matter more than taking the fastest route?
questions.explanation: Simon learned parcels had to fit around appointments and children returning home.

questions.question: mentions a problem caused by confusing two similar locations?
questions.explanation: Simon describes a volunteer going to the wrong similar address.

questions.question: had to collect information without making people feel judged?
questions.explanation: Iqra asked questions without sounding as if callers had to prove they deserved help.

questions.question: anticipated a need that someone was too embarrassed to mention first?
questions.explanation: Iqra mentioned baby formula before the caller could ask for it.

questions.question: learned that the sequence of loading affected how smoothly deliveries ran?
questions.explanation: Rob had to load first deliveries nearest the doors.

questions.question: caused a delay after damaging an item containing liquid?
questions.explanation: Rob broke a jar of pasta sauce, delaying deliveries.

questions.question: created multilingual written information for families sharing details?
questions.explanation: Iqra made translated notes about collection times.

questions.question: says privacy is protected by not knowing too much about recipients?
questions.explanation: Rob prefers not hearing the stories behind parcels because it protects privacy.
